text:
Single-atom catalysts featuring well-defined metal centers offer a unique platform for unraveling the fundamental mechanisms and active sites governing chemical transformations. Despite their potential, capturing the real-time electronic and structural dynamics of these centers under operating conditions remains a significant challenge, largely due to the difficulty of integrating site-specific *operando* techniques with ideal model systems. In this study, we employ a suite of sophisticated *operando* methodologies to investigate the dynamic evolution of a model catalyst consisting of high-spin (HS) Fe(III)N₄ centers during the electrochemical CO₂ reduction reaction (CO₂RR). 

Through *operando* ⁵⁷Fe Mössbauer and X-ray absorption spectroscopies, we demonstrate a potential-dependent transition from HS Fe(III)N₄ to HS Fe(II)N₄. This transition is further influenced by the electrolyte environment (CO₂- vs. Ar-saturation), which dictates the nature of the adsorbates and the relative stability of the Fe(II) state. Complementary *operando* Raman spectroscopy and cyclic voltammetry reveal that the phthalocyanine (Pc) ligand backbone undergoes a simultaneous redox transformation, shifting from Fe(II)Pc to a reduced Fe(II)Pc⁻ state. Our findings identify this HS Fe(II)Pc⁻ species as the critical catalytic intermediate for CO₂RR. Theoretical calculations further elucidate that the electroreduction of the Pc ligand shifts the d-band center of the *in situ* generated Fe(II) center, optimizing the binding affinity for CO₂ and dramatically enhancing catalytic activity. By providing a comprehensive experimental and theoretical map of the active site’s electronic structure, this work offers vital insights for the rational design of high-performance single-atom catalysts for CO₂ conversion.
